audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Nazareth rejection, sending the Twelve, Herod/John narrative, feeding five thousand, sea-walking, and Gennesaret healings.

verses.restored: He went out from there and came into his homeland, and his disciples followed him.

When Sabbath came, he began to teach in the synagogue, and many hearing were struck with wonder, saying, From where are these things to this one? What is this wisdom given to him, and such powers coming through his hands?
Is this not the craftsman, the son of Mary, brother of James, Joses, Judas, and Simon? Are not his sisters here with us? And they stumbled over him.
Jesus said to them, A prophet is not without honor except in his homeland, among his relatives, and in his house.
He was not able to do any power there, except that he laid hands on a few sick people and healed them.
He marveled because of their lack of trust. He went around the villages in a circle, teaching.
He called the twelve and began to send them out two by two, giving them authority over unclean spirits.
He instructed them to take nothing for the way except a staff: no bread, no bag, no copper in the belt,
but to wear sandals and not put on two tunics.
He said to them, Wherever you enter a house, remain there until you go out from there.
Whatever place does not receive you or hear you, as you go out from there, shake the dust from under your feet as a testimony to them.
They went out and proclaimed that people should turn/realign.
They cast out many unclean spirits, anointed many sick people with oil, and healed them.
King Herod heard, for Jesus' name had become known. Some were saying, John the Baptist has been raised from the dead, and because of this the powers are at work in him.
Others were saying, He is Elijah. Others were saying, A prophet like one of the prophets.
But Herod, hearing, said, John, whom I beheaded, has been raised.
For Herod himself had sent and seized John and bound him in prison because of Herodias, the wife of Philip his brother, because he had married her.
For John had been saying to Herod, It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife.
Herodias held it against him and wanted to kill him, but she was not able.
For Herod feared John, knowing him to be a righteous and holy man, and protected him. Hearing him, he was greatly perplexed, yet he heard him gladly.
An opportune day came when Herod, on his birthday, made a banquet for his nobles, military commanders, and the first people of Galilee.
When Herodias' daughter came in and danced, she pleased Herod and those reclining with him. The king said to the girl, Ask me whatever you wish, and I will give it to you.
He swore to her, Whatever you ask me, I will give you, up to half my reign.
She went out and said to her mother, What should I ask? She said, The head of John the Baptist.
Immediately she came in with haste to the king and asked, saying, I want you to give me at once the head of John the Baptist on a platter.
The king became deeply grieved, but because of the oaths and those reclining with him, he did not want to refuse her.
Immediately the king sent an executioner and commanded him to bring John's head. He went and beheaded him in the prison.
He brought his head on a platter and gave it to the girl, and the girl gave it to her mother.
When his disciples heard, they came and took his body and placed it in a tomb.
The apostles gathered to Jesus and reported to him all they had done and taught.
He said to them, Come away by yourselves to a deserted place and rest a little. For many were coming and going, and they had no opportunity even to eat.
They went away in the boat to a deserted place by themselves.
Many saw them going and recognized them; on foot from all the cities they ran together there and arrived ahead of them.
Coming out, he saw a great crowd and was moved with compassion for them, because they were like sheep not having a shepherd; and he began to teach them many things.
When it was already a late hour, his disciples came to him and said, This place is deserted, and the hour is already late.
Release them so they may go into the surrounding farms and villages and buy themselves something to eat.
But he answered, You give them something to eat. They said, Shall we go and buy two hundred denarii of bread and give them to eat?
He said to them, How many loaves do you have? Go see. Knowing, they said, Five, and two fish.
He instructed them to make all recline in groups on the green grass.
They reclined in groups of hundreds and fifties.
Taking the five loaves and two fish, looking up to heaven, he blessed, broke the loaves, and gave them to the disciples to set before them; and he divided the two fish among all.
All ate and were filled.
They took up twelve baskets full of broken pieces and of the fish.
Those who ate the loaves were five thousand men.
Immediately he compelled his disciples to enter the boat and go before him to the other side, toward Bethsaida, while he released the crowd.
After taking leave of them, he went away into the mountain to pray.
When evening came, the boat was in the middle of the sea, and he was alone on the land.
Seeing them distressed in rowing, for the wind was against them, about the fourth watch of the night he came to them, walking on the sea, and he intended to pass by them.
Seeing him walking on the sea, they thought he was a ghost/appearance and cried out.
For they all saw him and were troubled. Immediately he spoke with them and said, Take courage; I am. Do not fear.
He went up to them into the boat, and the wind ceased. They were exceedingly astonished in themselves.
For they did not understand about the loaves, but their heart had been hardened.
Crossing over, they came to land at Gennesaret and anchored.
When they came out of the boat, immediately people recognized him.
They ran around that whole region and began to carry the sick on mats to wherever they heard he was.
Wherever he entered villages, cities, or farms, they laid the sick in the marketplaces and pleaded that they might touch even the fringe of his garment; and as many as touched it were saved/healed.
